questions.stem: The circuit below flips only qubit 0 of a 3-qubit register and then measures every qubit with `measure_all()`. Running it on the default `AerSimulator`, which single bitstring is returned for every shot?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.x(0)
qc.measure_all()

explanation.correct: Qiskit prints bitstrings in little-endian order: the rightmost character is the classical bit holding qubit 0, and the index increases leftward. Only qubit 0 is excited, so the outcome is `001` (q2 q1 q0 = 0 0 1).

distractors.A: `100` treats the leftmost character as qubit 0 (big-endian). Qiskit is little-endian, so qubit 0 is the RIGHTMOST bit.
distractors.B: `010` is the outcome you would get if qubit 1 were flipped; the X acts on qubit 0.
distractors.D: `111` would require all three qubits to be excited; only qubit 0 has an X gate.

evidence.A: option claims '100'; observed 0/1000 shots gave '100'; full counts={'001': 1000}
evidence.B: option claims '010'; observed 0/1000 shots gave '010'; full counts={'001': 1000}
evidence.C: option claims '001'; observed 1000/1000 shots gave '001'; full counts={'001': 1000}
evidence.D: option claims '111'; observed 0/1000 shots gave '111'; full counts={'001': 1000}

questions.stem: A 2-qubit circuit is created with `QuantumCircuit(2)` (no classical register). Which statement correctly describes what `qc.measure_all()` does to it?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.measure_all()

options.text: It raises an error because the circuit has no classical bits to measure into.
It adds a new 2-bit classical register named `meas` plus a barrier, then measures each qubit into the matching new bit.
It adds 2 classical bits to a register named `c` and inserts no barrier.
It measures only qubits that already carry gates, adding a single classical bit.

explanation.correct: `measure_all()` creates a fresh `ClassicalRegister` named `meas` sized to the number of qubits, inserts a `barrier` across all qubits, and measures qubit i into `meas[i]`. It is the convenient one-call way to read out an entire circuit.

distractors.A: `measure_all()` never errors on a register-less circuit — it creates the classical bits for you (unlike `measure`, which needs existing bits).
distractors.C: The auto-created register is named `meas`, not `c`, and `measure_all()` DOES insert a barrier before the measurements by default.
distractors.D: Measuring only active qubits is what `measure_active()` does; `measure_all()` measures every qubit.

evidence.A: cregs=['meas'], num_clbits=2, barrier=True, ops={'measure': 2, 'h': 1, 'cx': 1, 'barrier': 1}: measure_all did not raise -> claim(error) is False
evidence.B: cregs=['meas'], num_clbits=2, barrier=True, ops={'measure': 2, 'h': 1, 'cx': 1, 'barrier': 1}: register 'meas' size2 + barrier + 2 measures -> True
evidence.C: cregs=['meas'], num_clbits=2, barrier=True, ops={'measure': 2, 'h': 1, 'cx': 1, 'barrier': 1}: register is 'meas' not 'c' and barrier present -> False
evidence.D: cregs=['meas'], num_clbits=2, barrier=True, ops={'measure': 2, 'h': 1, 'cx': 1, 'barrier': 1}: all qubits measured, num_clbits=2 not 1 -> False

questions.stem: `sub` (a 2-qubit circuit whose only gate is `cx(0, 1)`) is composed onto the 3-qubit circuit `qc` with the mapping `qubits=[0, 2]`. In the resulting circuit `comp`, which qubits does the CX act on?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.h(0)

sub = QuantumCircuit(2)
sub.cx(0, 1)

comp = qc.compose(sub, qubits=[0, 2])

options.text: Control = qubit 0, target = qubit 1
Control = qubit 1, target = qubit 2
Control = qubit 0, target = qubit 2
`compose` raises an error because `sub` has fewer qubits than `qc`

explanation.correct: `compose(sub, qubits=[0, 2])` maps `sub`'s qubit 0 to `qc`'s qubit 0 and `sub`'s qubit 1 to `qc`'s qubit 2. The CX `sub.cx(0, 1)` therefore becomes a CX with control qubit 0 and target qubit 2 in `comp`.

distractors.A: Target qubit 1 ignores the `qubits=[0, 2]` mapping and assumes the sub-circuit lands on qubits 0 and 1.
distractors.B: This misreads the mapping as shifting both qubits up by one; the mapping sends sub-qubit 0 to physical qubit 0.
distractors.D: Composing a smaller circuit onto a subset of a larger one is exactly what the `qubits` argument is for — no error occurs.

evidence.B: observed CX=(0,2); claim (1,2) -> False
evidence.C: observed CX (control,target)=(0,2); claim (0,2) -> True
evidence.D: compose succeeded, comp ops={'h': 1, 'cx': 1}; no error -> False

questions.stem: A developer expects `base` to contain its H followed by the X from `add_on`, but after this code `base` still shows only the H gate. What is the bug and the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

base = QuantumCircuit(1)
base.h(0)

add_on = QuantumCircuit(1)
add_on.x(0)

base.compose(add_on)   # intended to append X onto base
print(base.count_ops())

options.text: `compose` cannot be used for single-qubit gates; `base.append` must be used instead.
`compose` returns a new circuit and does not modify `base` in place; fix with `base = base.compose(add_on)` or `base.compose(add_on, inplace=True)`.
`add_on` must be converted with `add_on.to_gate()` before it can be composed.
`base` needs a classical register before `compose` takes effect.

explanation.correct: By default `compose` is functional: it RETURNS a new circuit and leaves the caller untouched (`inplace=False`). Discarding the return value loses the X. Assign the result back, or pass `inplace=True` to mutate `base`.

distractors.A: `compose` works fine for single-qubit gates — the problem is only that the returned circuit was discarded; `append` is not required.
distractors.C: `to_gate()` is optional; you can compose whole circuits directly. It is unrelated to the in-place behavior.
distractors.D: Classical registers have nothing to do with composing gates; the X is lost purely because the return value was ignored.

evidence.A: compose produced {'h', 'x'} without append -> 'must use append' is False
evidence.B: buggy base ops={'h'}; after reassign={'h', 'x'}; after inplace=True={'h', 'x'} -> True
evidence.C: compose succeeded without to_gate() (ops={'h', 'x'}) -> False
evidence.D: no classical register was added; ops={'h', 'x'} -> False

questions.stem: You want a circuit with a 2-qubit register `q` and a 2-bit register `c`, both qubits in superposition, measuring `q[i]` into `c[i]`. Which snippet builds AND runs on `AerSimulator` without raising, producing 2-bit outcomes?

options.text: `qc=QuantumCircuit(2); qc.h([0,1]); qc.measure([0,1],[0,1])`
`q=QuantumRegister(2,'q'); qc=QuantumCircuit(q); qc.h(q); qc.measure(q,q)`
`q=QuantumRegister(2,'q'); c=ClassicalRegister(2,'c'); qc=QuantumCircuit(q,c); qc.h(q); qc.measure(c,q)`
`q=QuantumRegister(2,'q'); c=ClassicalRegister(2,'c'); qc=QuantumCircuit(q,c); qc.h(q[0]); qc.h(q[1]); qc.measure(q[0],c[0]); qc.measure(q[1],c[1])`

explanation.correct: You must declare classical bits before measuring into them. Option D creates both registers, passes them to `QuantumCircuit`, and calls `measure(qubit, clbit)` in the correct argument order, so it builds and runs, giving 2-bit outcomes.

distractors.A: `QuantumCircuit(2)` has NO classical bits, so `measure([0,1],[0,1])` has nowhere to store results and raises a `CircuitError`.
distractors.B: `measure(q, q)` passes qubits as the classical-bit argument; measurement targets must be classical bits, so this raises.
distractors.C: `measure(c, q)` swaps the argument order — `measure` expects `(qubit, clbit)`, not `(clbit, qubit)` — and raises.

evidence.A: raised CircuitError: 'Index 0 out of range for size 0.'
evidence.B: raised CircuitError: "Incorrect bit type: expected 'B' but got 'Qubit'"
evidence.C: raised CircuitError: "Incorrect bit type: expected 'B' but got 'Clbit'"
evidence.D: built and ran; outcome bit-width=2, sample counts={'01': 54, '11': 42, '10': 48, '00': 56} -> valid=True

questions.stem: Consider the circuit below. Select ALL statements that are TRUE about its structural properties. (Recall `width()` counts qubits + classical bits.)
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister

qr = QuantumRegister(3, 'q')
cr = ClassicalRegister(2, 'c')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, cr)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.cx(1, 2)
qc.measure(0, 0)
qc.measure(2, 1)

options.text: The circuit has 3 qubits and 2 classical bits.
`qc.width()` returns 3.
`qc.size()` returns 5 (the total number of operations).
`qc.depth()` equals `qc.size()`.
`qc.num_qubits + qc.num_clbits` equals `qc.width()`.

explanation.correct: `num_qubits`=3 and `num_clbits`=2 (A). `size()` counts all 5 operations: 1 H + 2 CX + 2 measures (C). `width()` returns qubits + classical bits = 3 + 2 = 5, so it equals `num_qubits + num_clbits` (E).

distractors.B: `width()` returns qubits PLUS classical bits (5 here), not the qubit count. Confusing `width()` with `num_qubits` is a common slip.
distractors.D: `depth()` is the longest gate path (4 here), while `size()` is the total operation count (5); they are different metrics and are not equal for this circuit.

evidence.A: num_qubits=3, num_clbits=2, width=5, size=5, depth=4: qubits=3 & clbits=2 -> True
evidence.B: num_qubits=3, num_clbits=2, width=5, size=5, depth=4: width()=5 (qubits+clbits), not 3 -> False
evidence.C: num_qubits=3, num_clbits=2, width=5, size=5, depth=4: size()=5 operations -> True
evidence.D: num_qubits=3, num_clbits=2, width=5, size=5, depth=4: depth=4 vs size=5 -> equal? False
evidence.E: num_qubits=3, num_clbits=2, width=5, size=5, depth=4: num_qubits+num_clbits=5 vs width=5 -> True

questions.stem: The circuit prepares a 3-qubit state. Which computational-basis states have non-zero amplitude in its statevector, and with what amplitude?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.cx(0, 2)

sv = Statevector(qc)

options.text: `|000⟩` and `|111⟩`, each with amplitude 1/√2 (a GHZ state).
`|000⟩` and `|100⟩`, each with amplitude 1/√2.
All eight basis states, each with amplitude 1/√8.
`|000⟩` and `|011⟩`, each with amplitude 1/√2.

explanation.correct: H on qubit 0 makes (|0⟩+|1⟩)/√2. The two CX gates copy qubit 0's value onto qubits 1 and 2, so the state becomes (|000⟩ + |111⟩)/√2 — a 3-qubit GHZ state with only those two basis states populated.

distractors.B: `|100⟩` would appear only if the CX gates did nothing; they entangle qubits 1 and 2 with qubit 0, flipping them when qubit 0 is 1.
distractors.C: A uniform superposition over all 8 states needs an H on every qubit; here only qubit 0 gets an H and the CXs create correlation, not full superposition.
distractors.D: `|011⟩` misplaces the excitation — both CX gates are controlled by qubit 0, so all three qubits share the same value (000 or 111).

evidence.A: nonzero basis states = {'000': (0.7071+0j), '111': (0.7071+0j)}; claim {000,111} at 1/sqrt2 -> True
evidence.C: nonzero = {'000': (0.7071+0j), '111': (0.7071+0j)} (2 states); claim all-8-uniform -> False

questions.stem: A list of values is passed to `assign_parameters`. What angles do the `rx` and `rz` gates end up with?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

theta = Parameter('theta')
phi = Parameter('phi')

qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rx(theta, 0)   # added first
qc.rz(phi, 0)     # added second

bound = qc.assign_parameters([0.5, 1.5])

options.text: `rx = 0.5`, `rz = 1.5` — the list is applied in the order the gates were added.
`rx = 0.5`, `rz = 0.5` — both gates receive the first value.
It raises an error: `assign_parameters` requires a dictionary, not a list.
`rx = 1.5`, `rz = 0.5` — the list is applied in alphabetical parameter order (`phi`, then `theta`).

explanation.correct: When you pass a plain list, values are bound to `qc.parameters`, which is sorted ALPHABETICALLY by name — `phi` before `theta`. So `phi`=0.5 and `theta`=1.5, giving `rz`(phi)=0.5 and `rx`(theta)=1.5. Insertion order is irrelevant.

distractors.A: This assumes the list follows the order gates were added. It actually follows `qc.parameters`, which is alphabetical (`phi` < `theta`).
distractors.B: Each parameter gets a distinct list entry; they are not both set to the first value.
distractors.C: A list is a valid argument — it binds positionally to the sorted parameters. A dict is only required for partial binding.

evidence.A: qc.parameters order=['phi', 'theta']; observed rx=1.5, rz=0.5; claim rx0.5/rz1.5 -> False
evidence.B: observed rx=1.5, rz=0.5; claim both 0.5 -> False
evidence.C: assign_parameters([0.5,1.5]) succeeded (no error); claim 'needs dict' -> False
evidence.D: parameters sorted=['phi', 'theta']; observed rx=1.5, rz=0.5; claim rx1.5/rz0.5 -> True

questions.stem: Three parameters are created and added to a circuit in the order `gamma`, `alpha`, `beta`. What does `[p.name for p in qc.parameters]` return?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

g = Parameter('gamma'); a = Parameter('alpha'); b = Parameter('beta')
qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rz(g, 0)
qc.rx(a, 0)
qc.ry(b, 0)

print([p.name for p in qc.parameters])

options.text: `['alpha', 'beta', 'gamma']` — sorted alphabetically by name.
`['gamma', 'alpha', 'beta']` — the order the parameters were added.
`['beta', 'alpha', 'gamma']` — reverse insertion order.
The order is unspecified and can vary between runs.

explanation.correct: `QuantumCircuit.parameters` returns a `ParameterView` sorted alphabetically by parameter name, so it is `['alpha', 'beta', 'gamma']`. This deterministic ordering is exactly why passing a bare list to `assign_parameters` can surprise you.

distractors.B: Parameters are NOT kept in insertion order; they are sorted by name.
distractors.C: The ordering is alphabetical ascending, not reverse insertion order.
distractors.D: The order is fully deterministic (alphabetical), not run-dependent.

evidence.B: observed order=['alpha', 'beta', 'gamma']; claim insertion order -> False
evidence.C: observed order=['alpha', 'beta', 'gamma']; claim reverse insertion -> False
evidence.D: observed order=['alpha', 'beta', 'gamma'] is deterministic across runs; claim 'unspecified' -> False

questions.stem: This code is meant to bind both parameters but raises a `ValueError`. What is the problem and the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

a = Parameter('a'); b = Parameter('b')
qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rx(a, 0)
qc.rz(b, 0)

bound = qc.assign_parameters([0.7])   # intended to bind a and b

options.text: `assign_parameters` only accepts a dictionary; a list is never allowed.
A list must provide a value for EVERY parameter; supply `[0.7, <b_value>]`, or use a dict like `{a: 0.7}` for partial binding.
Parameters must be bound individually with `gate.assign()`.
The circuit must be transpiled before any parameters can be assigned.

explanation.correct: A list binds positionally to ALL of `qc.parameters`, so its length must equal the number of parameters (2 here). Passing one value raises `ValueError`. Provide both values in a list, or pass a dict to bind a subset.

distractors.A: A list IS accepted when its length matches the parameter count; the error is the wrong length, not the list type.
distractors.C: There is no per-gate `gate.assign()` workflow; binding is done on the circuit via `assign_parameters`.
distractors.D: Parameter assignment is independent of transpilation; you can (and usually do) bind before transpiling.

evidence.A: a length-2 list bound successfully (num_params after=0) -> 'list never allowed' is False
evidence.B: one value raised [ValueError: Mismatching number of values and parameters. For partial binding please pass a m]; [0.7,1.2] bound all (True); dict {a:0.7} left 1 free (True) -> True
evidence.C: binding is done via circuit.assign_parameters, not per-gate -> False
evidence.D: assignment worked with no transpilation -> False

questions.stem: After this binding step, which statement about `qc` and `bound` is correct?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

theta = Parameter('theta')
qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rx(theta, 0)

bound = qc.assign_parameters({theta: 3.14})

options.text: `qc` is now fully bound (0 free parameters), and `bound` is the same object as `qc`.
`assign_parameters` returns `None`; the bound circuit must be read back from `qc`.
`qc` still has 1 free parameter, and `bound` is a new circuit with 0 free parameters.
Both `qc` and `bound` have 0 free parameters, because `assign_parameters` mutates in place by default.

explanation.correct: `assign_parameters` is functional by default (`inplace=False`): it returns a NEW circuit with the values substituted and leaves the original untouched. So `qc` keeps its 1 free parameter while `bound` has 0.

distractors.A: `bound` is a distinct new circuit, not the same object as `qc`; and `qc` is not modified.
distractors.B: `assign_parameters` returns the bound circuit, not `None` — that is why you capture it in `bound`.
distractors.D: The default is `inplace=False`, so `qc` is NOT mutated; you would need `inplace=True` for that.

evidence.A: qc.num_parameters=1, bound.num_parameters=0, bound is qc=False: bound is a separate object and qc keeps its parameter -> False
evidence.B: assign_parameters returned a circuit (is None=False) -> False
evidence.C: qc.num_parameters=1, bound.num_parameters=0, bound is qc=False: original unchanged, new circuit fully bound -> True
evidence.D: qc.num_parameters=1, bound.num_parameters=0, bound is qc=False: qc was NOT mutated (default inplace=False) -> False

questions.stem: The gates use parameter EXPRESSIONS. After binding `t = π`, what numeric angles do `rx` and `rz` receive?
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

t = Parameter('t')
qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rx(2 * t, 0)
qc.rz(t + np.pi / 2, 0)

bound = qc.assign_parameters({t: np.pi})

options.text: `rx = π ≈ 3.142`, `rz = π ≈ 3.142` — the expressions are ignored and `t` is substituted directly.
`rx = 2π ≈ 6.283`, `rz = π/2 ≈ 1.571` — only the coefficient is applied; the added constant is dropped.
It raises an error because gate angles must be plain `Parameter`s, not expressions.

explanation.correct: Parameter expressions are evaluated at bind time. With `t = π`: `2*t = 2π ≈ 6.283`, and `t + π/2 = 3π/2 ≈ 4.712`. Qiskit fully supports arithmetic on `Parameter` objects inside gate arguments.

distractors.B: The coefficient and additive constant are part of the expression and ARE applied; `t` is not substituted raw.
distractors.C: The additive constant `π/2` is not dropped — `rz` gets `π + π/2 = 3π/2`.
distractors.D: Expressions like `2*t` and `t + π/2` are valid gate arguments; no error occurs.

evidence.B: observed rx=6.2832, rz=4.7124; claim both=pi -> False
evidence.C: observed rz=4.7124; claim rz=pi/2=1.5708 (constant dropped) -> False
evidence.D: binding expressions succeeded (rx=6.2832, rz=4.7124); claim 'error' -> False

questions.stem: A circuit is built with a `ParameterVector`. Select ALL true statements.
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import ParameterVector

x = ParameterVector('x', 3)
qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
for i in range(3):
    qc.ry(x[i], i)

options.text: `qc.num_parameters` is 3.
The parameters are named `x[0]`, `x[1]`, `x[2]`.
`qc.assign_parameters({x: [0.1, 0.2, 0.3]})` binds the entire vector in one call.
`x[0]` and `x[1]` are the same `Parameter` object.
A `ParameterVector` can only be bound one element at a time, never as a whole.

explanation.correct: A `ParameterVector('x', 3)` creates 3 distinct parameters named `x[0..2]` (A, B). You can bind the whole vector at once by using it as a single dict key mapped to a list of values (C).

distractors.D: Each index is a distinct `Parameter`; `x[0]` and `x[1]` are different objects that bind to different values.
distractors.E: A `ParameterVector` can be bound in one call (`{x: [...]}`) OR element by element — not only element-by-element.

evidence.B: parameter names=['x[0]', 'x[1]', 'x[2]'] -> True
evidence.C: assign_parameters({x:[...]}) left 0 free -> whole-vector bind works True
evidence.D: x[0] is x[1] -> False
evidence.E: whole-vector bind succeeded (free=0) so element-by-element is NOT the only way -> False

questions.stem: This dynamic circuit measures qubit 0 and, only if that result is 1, applies X to qubit 1; then it measures qubit 1. Over many shots (bit order `c1 c0`), which outcomes appear?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister

qr = QuantumRegister(2, 'q')
cr = ClassicalRegister(2, 'c')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, cr)
qc.h(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)
with qc.if_test((cr[0], 1)):
    qc.x(1)
qc.measure(1, 1)

options.text: Only `01` and `00`, roughly 50/50.
All four of `00`, `01`, `10`, `11`, roughly 25% each.
Only `10` and `11`, roughly 50/50.
Only `00` and `11`, roughly 50/50 — qubit 1 always ends up matching qubit 0.

explanation.correct: The H puts qubit 0 into an equal superposition, so its measurement `c0` is 0 or 1 with ~50% each. The feedforward flips qubit 1 exactly when `c0`=1, so `c1` always equals `c0`. The only outcomes are `00` and `11`.

distractors.A: `01` would need `c1 != c0`; the X is applied precisely when `c0`=1, keeping the two bits equal.
distractors.B: Qubit 1 is not independent — it is conditioned on qubit 0's result, so the outcomes are correlated, not uniform.
distractors.C: `10` requires qubit 1 to be 1 while qubit 0 is 0, but X is only applied when `c0`=1.

evidence.B: observed outcomes={'00': 2000, '11': 2000}; claim all four -> False
evidence.C: observed outcomes={'00': 2000, '11': 2000}; claim {10,11} -> False

questions.stem: After measuring qubit 0 into `cr[0]`, you want to apply X to qubit 1 only when that bit equals 1. Which snippet is valid in Qiskit 2.x and produces a conditional (`if_else`) operation?
questions.code:
# qr = QuantumRegister(2,'q'); cr = ClassicalRegister(2,'c')
# qc has already done: qc.h(0); qc.measure(0, 0)

options.text:
`with qc.if_test(cr[0], 1):`
    `qc.x(1)`

`with qc.if_test((cr[0], 1)):`
    `qc.x(1)`
`qc.if_test((cr[0], 1), qc.x, [1])`

explanation.correct: The modern control-flow API is a context manager whose single argument is a `(classical_resource, value)` TUPLE: `with qc.if_test((cr[0], 1)):`. Gates added inside the block become the conditional body, producing an `if_else` operation.

distractors.A: `.c_if()` was REMOVED in Qiskit 2.0; calling it raises `AttributeError`. This is the classic v1-to-v2 migration trap.
distractors.B: `if_test` takes ONE positional argument (the condition tuple); passing `cr[0], 1` as two arguments raises a `TypeError`.
distractors.D: There is no `(condition, callable, qubits)` form; used without a `with` block and body qubits it raises a `CircuitError`.

evidence.A: raised AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'c_if'
evidence.B: raised TypeError: 'qiskit.circuit.Clbit' object is not subscriptable
evidence.C: built successfully; ops={'h': 1, 'measure': 1, 'if_else': 1}; has if_else=True
evidence.D: raised CircuitError: "When using 'if_test' with a body, you must pass qubits and clbits."

questions.stem: This conditional code was written for an older Qiskit release and now fails with `AttributeError` on Qiskit 2.x. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister

qr = QuantumRegister(1, 'q')
cr = ClassicalRegister(1, 'c')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, cr)
qc.h(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)
qc.x(0).c_if(cr, 1)   # AttributeError on Qiskit 2.x

options.text: Replace it with a context manager: `with qc.if_test((cr, 1)):` then `qc.x(0)`. `.c_if()` was removed in Qiskit 2.0.
Change `c_if(cr, 1)` to `c_if(cr[0], 1)`; the condition must reference a single bit.
Call `qc = qc.decompose()` before the conditional so `.c_if()` becomes available.
Use `qc.x(0, condition=(cr, 1))`; the `condition` keyword replaces `.c_if()`.

explanation.correct: The `InstructionSet.c_if` method was removed in Qiskit 2.0. Conditionals are now built with the control-flow context manager `with qc.if_test((cr, 1)):`, whose body holds the conditioned gates.

distractors.B: `.c_if()` no longer exists at all — indexing the register to `cr[0]` still raises `AttributeError`; the method itself is gone.
distractors.C: `decompose()` expands composite gates and has nothing to do with conditionals; it does not restore `.c_if()`.
distractors.D: Gate methods like `x()` have no `condition` keyword; passing one raises a `TypeError`. Conditions go through `if_test`.

evidence.A: bug=AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'c_if'; fix with if_test -> ops={'h': 1, 'measure': 1, 'if_else': 1} -> valid conditional True
evidence.B: c_if(cr[0],1) -> raised AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'c_if' -> False
evidence.C: decompose then c_if -> raised AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'c_if' -> False

questions.stem: This dynamic circuit branches on `cr[0]`: if it is 0 it applies X to qubit 1, ELSE it applies Z to qubit 1; then it measures qubit 1. Which outcomes appear (bit order `c1 c0`)?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister

qr = QuantumRegister(2, 'q')
cr = ClassicalRegister(2, 'c')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, cr)
qc.h(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)
with qc.if_test((cr[0], 0)) as else_:
    qc.x(1)
with else_:
    qc.z(1)
qc.measure(1, 1)

options.text: Only `00` and `11`, roughly 50/50.
Only `10` and `01`, roughly 50/50.
Only `11` and `01`, roughly 50/50.
All four outcomes, roughly 25% each.

explanation.correct: When `c0`=0 the X flips qubit 1 to `|1⟩`, so `c1`=1 → `10`. When `c0`=1 the Z acts on `|0⟩` and leaves it unchanged, so `c1`=0 → `01`. The two branches give `10` and `01`, each ~50%.

distractors.A: `00`/`11` would require the two bits to match; here the X-branch makes them differ and the Z-branch leaves qubit 1 at 0 while `c0`=1, so they always differ.
distractors.C: `11` never occurs: qubit 1 becomes 1 only in the `c0`=0 branch, which pairs with `c0`=0, giving `10`.
distractors.D: The outcome of qubit 1 is fully determined by `c0`, so only two of the four combinations occur.

evidence.D: observed={'10': 2000, '01': 2000}; claim all four -> False

questions.stem: Which scenario genuinely REQUIRES a dynamic circuit (a mid-circuit measurement whose classical result feeds forward to control a later gate), rather than a plain static circuit?

options.text: Applying a fixed sequence of gates to all qubits and measuring only at the very end.
Sweeping a rotation angle across many values to plot an expectation curve.
Reducing two-qubit gate count by letting the transpiler cancel adjacent gates.
Measuring an ancilla mid-circuit and applying a correction gate to a data qubit only when that measurement is 1.

explanation.correct: Dynamic circuits exist precisely for real-time classical feedforward: measure a qubit partway through, then condition subsequent quantum operations on that classical outcome (as in teleportation corrections or error-correction syndrome handling). Option D is exactly that pattern.

distractors.A: Measuring only at the end with a fixed gate sequence is a static circuit — no feedforward is involved.
distractors.B: Parameter sweeps are handled by parameterized circuits and array broadcasting, not by classical feedforward.
distractors.C: Cancelling adjacent gates is a transpiler optimization; it needs no measurement-conditioned control flow.

questions.stem: The `for_loop` below repeats its body over `range(3)`, applying X to qubit 0 three times, then measures. What does the measurement return?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(1, 1)
with qc.for_loop(range(3)):
    qc.x(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)

options.text: Always `0` — the repeated X gates cancel out to the identity.
It raises an error because `for_loop` requires a classical register argument.
Always `1` — X is applied an odd number of times (3), flipping `|0⟩` to `|1⟩`.
`0` and `1` with roughly 50% probability each.

explanation.correct: The loop body runs 3 times, so X is applied 3 times. Three X gates equal a single X (odd count), flipping `|0⟩` to `|1⟩`, so every shot measures `1`.

distractors.A: Three X gates do not cancel — an even count cancels to identity, but 3 is odd, leaving one net flip.
distractors.B: `for_loop` takes an index set like `range(3)`; it does not require a classical register.
distractors.D: There is no superposition here — X is deterministic, so the outcome is a definite `1`, not 50/50.

evidence.B: for_loop(range(3)) built and ran without error -> claim 'error' is False
evidence.C: observed counts={'1': 1000}; all shots = '1' -> True
evidence.D: observed counts={'1': 1000}; claim ~50/50 -> False

questions.stem: Select ALL statements that correctly describe control flow in Qiskit 2.x.

options.text: `if_test` is used as a context manager whose condition is a `(classical_resource, value)` tuple.
`.c_if()` is still available on gate instructions as the recommended way to add a conditional.
Control-flow blocks such as `if_else` and `for_loop` appear as single operations in `count_ops()`.
A `for_loop` body may contain at most one gate.
A `switch` block can define a fallback branch using `case(case.DEFAULT)`.

explanation.correct: `if_test((resource, value))` is the context-manager API (A). Each control-flow block is stored as one composite instruction, so `count_ops()` shows `if_else`, `for_loop`, etc. (C). `switch` supports a default branch via `case(case.DEFAULT)` (E).

distractors.B: `.c_if()` was REMOVED in Qiskit 2.0; the supported API is the `if_test` context manager, not `.c_if()`.
distractors.D: A `for_loop` body can contain any number of gates, not just one.

evidence.C: for_loop op count = {'for_loop': 1} -> single-op block True

print(pm.stages)

options.text: `('layout', 'routing', 'translation', 'optimization')` — four stages.
`('init', 'layout', 'routing', 'optimization', 'measurement')`.
`('sampling', 'layout', 'routing', 'translation', 'scheduling')`.
`('init', 'layout', 'routing', 'translation', 'optimization', 'scheduling')` — six stages.

explanation.correct: A preset `StagedPassManager` runs six stages in order: init, layout, routing, translation, optimization, and scheduling. Layout+routing map the circuit to hardware qubits/connectivity, translation rewrites to the basis gates, optimization simplifies, and scheduling adds timing.

distractors.A: This omits the `init` and `scheduling` stages; the preset pipeline has six named stages, not four.
distractors.B: `measurement` is not a transpiler stage, and this list drops `translation` and `scheduling`.
distractors.C: `sampling` is a runtime/primitive concept, not a transpiler stage; the pipeline never contains it.

explanation.correct: `generate_preset_pass_manager` defaults to `optimization_level=2` — a balanced level with light-weight but effective optimization. (Note this differs from the older `transpile()` function, whose default resolves to level 1.)

distractors.A: Level 0 is minimal (only what is needed to run) and is not the default; you must ask for it explicitly.
distractors.C: Level 1 is the default of the legacy `transpile()` function, not of `generate_preset_pass_manager`.
distractors.D: Level 3 is the heaviest optimization and is never applied unless you request it.

questions.stem: A circuit whose only gate is `cx(0, 4)` is transpiled onto a 5-qubit LINE backend (connectivity 0-1-2-3-4, so qubits 0 and 4 are NOT directly connected). Comparing `optimization_level=0` with `optimization_level=1`, what happens to the number of CX gates?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.transpiler import CouplingMap
from qiskit.transpiler.preset_passmanagers import generate_preset_pass_manager
from qiskit.providers.fake_provider import GenericBackendV2

backend = GenericBackendV2(5, coupling_map=CouplingMap.from_line(5), seed=1)
qc = QuantumCircuit(5)
qc.cx(0, 4)

for lvl in (0, 1):
    isa = generate_preset_pass_manager(optimization_level=lvl,
                                       backend=backend, seed_transpiler=1).run(qc)
    print(lvl, isa.count_ops())

options.text: Level 0 needs many CX gates (it keeps the trivial layout and routes with SWAPs), while level 1 chooses a layout placing the two qubits adjacently, needing just one CX.
Both levels produce exactly one CX; the optimization level does not influence qubit layout.
Level 0 produces one CX; level 1 inserts SWAPs and ends up with more CX gates.
Both levels raise an error because a CX between non-adjacent qubits cannot be routed on a line.

explanation.correct: At level 0 the layout is trivial (virtual qubit i -> physical qubit i), so the CX between physical 0 and 4 must be routed with SWAPs, decomposed into many CX gates. At level 1 the layout pass picks two adjacent physical qubits for the interacting virtual qubits, so a single CX suffices. This is why layout/optimization level dramatically affects gate count.

distractors.B: Layout selection is part of the optimization level: level 0 uses a trivial layout while level 1+ actively searches for a good one, so the CX counts differ.
distractors.C: It is the other way around — the higher level reduces the count by choosing a better layout; level 0 is the expensive one here.
distractors.D: Routing on a line always succeeds by inserting SWAPs; it never raises for a single CX.

evidence.A: level0 cx=10, level1 cx=1; many-vs-one -> True
evidence.B: level0 cx=10, level1 cx=1; both==1? -> False
evidence.C: level0 cx=10, level1 cx=1; level1 more? -> False
evidence.D: both levels transpiled without error (cx: 10, 1) -> False

questions.stem: A backend's target has basis gates {cx, rz, sx, x} on a linear coupling map (qubit i connected only to i-1 and i+1). Which circuit is ALREADY an ISA circuit for this backend — needing no further transpilation?

options.text: A circuit using only `rz`, `sx`, and `cx`, with every `cx` acting on a physically-adjacent qubit pair.
A circuit that applies an `h` gate to qubit 0.
A circuit with a `cx` between qubit 0 and qubit 4 (not adjacent on the line).
A circuit containing a `ccx` (Toffoli) gate.

explanation.correct: An ISA (Instruction Set Architecture) circuit uses ONLY the backend's basis gates AND respects its coupling map. Option A satisfies both: `rz`/`sx`/`cx` are in the basis and each `cx` is on connected qubits, so no translation or routing is needed.

distractors.B: `h` is not in the basis {cx, rz, sx, x}; a translation pass must rewrite it, so the circuit is not yet ISA.
distractors.C: A `cx` on non-adjacent qubits violates the coupling map; the router must insert SWAPs first, so it is not ISA.
distractors.D: `ccx` is neither a basis gate nor a two-qubit gate; it must be decomposed and routed, so it is not ISA.

evidence.A: is_isa -> True (only basis gates on adjacent qubits)
evidence.B: is_isa -> False (h not in basis ['cx', 'delay', 'id', 'measure', 'reset', 'rz', 'sx', 'x'])
evidence.C: is_isa -> False (cx on non-adjacent (0, 4))
evidence.D: is_isa -> False (ccx not in basis ['cx', 'delay', 'id', 'measure', 'reset', 'rz', 'sx', 'x'])

questions.stem: This code transpiles a 4-qubit circuit for a 3-qubit backend and raises a `TranspilerError`. What is the root cause?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.transpiler.preset_passmanagers import generate_preset_pass_manager
from qiskit.providers.fake_provider import GenericBackendV2

backend = GenericBackendV2(num_qubits=3, seed=42)
qc = QuantumCircuit(4)
qc.h(range(4))
qc.measure_all()

pm = generate_preset_pass_manager(optimization_level=1, backend=backend)
isa = pm.run(qc)   # raises TranspilerError

options.text: `measure_all()` must be called AFTER transpilation, not before.
The circuit uses 4 qubits but the backend has only 3 — a circuit cannot be mapped onto a device with fewer qubits.
`optimization_level=1` is too low to map 4 qubits; raising it to level 3 fixes it.
`GenericBackendV2` must be constructed with an explicit `coupling_map` or transpilation always fails.

explanation.correct: Transpilation maps each virtual qubit onto a distinct physical qubit. With 4 virtual qubits and only 3 physical qubits, no valid layout exists, so the layout stage raises `TranspilerError: Number of qubits greater than device.` The fix is a backend with at least 4 qubits.

distractors.A: The position of `measure_all()` is irrelevant to the qubit-count mismatch; the error occurs during layout, not measurement.
distractors.C: No optimization level can invent extra physical qubits; higher levels still fail on the same mismatch.
distractors.D: `GenericBackendV2` supplies a default coupling map; the failure is the qubit count, not a missing coupling map.

evidence.A: removing measure_all still fails on 3q backend (fixed=False) -> False
evidence.B: bug=TranspilerError: 'Number of qubits greater than device.'; a 4-qubit backend transpiles fine (ops={'rz': 8, 'sx': 4, 'measure': 4, 'barrier': 1}) -> True
evidence.C: optimization_level=3 on 3q backend still fails (fixed=False) -> False
evidence.D: GenericBackendV2 had a default coupling map; the real cause is qubit count -> False

questions.stem: Select ALL statements that are TRUE about transpiler optimization levels 0-3.

options.text: Level 0 does the minimum needed to make the circuit runnable on the target, with essentially no optimization.
Level 3 applies the most aggressive optimization and typically takes the longest to run.
A higher optimization level is guaranteed to produce fewer two-qubit gates for every circuit.
`generate_preset_pass_manager` uses optimization level 1 by default.
All four levels target the same basis gates and coupling constraints; they differ in how hard they work to optimize.

explanation.correct: Level 0 is minimal mapping/translation with no real optimization (A). Level 3 is the most aggressive and usually the slowest (B). Every level produces a circuit valid for the SAME target constraints — the difference is optimization effort, not the constraints (E).

distractors.C: Optimization uses heuristics; a higher level USUALLY helps but is NOT guaranteed to beat a lower level on every circuit.
distractors.D: `generate_preset_pass_manager` defaults to level 2, not level 1. (Level 1 is the legacy `transpile()` default.)

questions.stem: The circuit has `h` and `t` gates on qubit 0. After transpiling to a `GenericBackendV2` whose basis is {cx, id, rz, sx, x, ...}, which statement about the resulting ISA circuit is TRUE?
questions.code:

from qiskit.providers.fake_provider import GenericBackendV2

backend = GenericBackendV2(num_qubits=5, seed=11)

qc.t(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)

isa = generate_preset_pass_manager(1, backend, seed_transpiler=11).run(qc)
print(isa.count_ops())

options.text: It still contains a `t` gate, because `t` is a standard gate kept as-is.
It keeps the `h` gate but the `t` gate is removed entirely.
Neither `h` nor `t` survives; both are rewritten into the target basis (e.g. `rz`/`sx`).
Transpilation raises an error because `t` is not one of the basis gates.

explanation.correct: The translation stage rewrites every gate into the target's basis. Since neither `h` nor `t` is a basis gate, both are decomposed into `rz`/`sx` (and the two-qubit `cx` stays), so the ISA circuit contains no `h` or `t`.

distractors.A: `t` is not in this backend's basis, so it cannot survive — it is translated away.
distractors.B: `h` is also absent from the basis and is likewise rewritten; it does not remain either.
distractors.D: A gate outside the basis is translated, not rejected — transpilation succeeds without error.

questions.stem: In the preset transpilation pipeline, which stage inserts SWAP gates so that every two-qubit gate acts on a physically-connected pair of qubits?

options.text: The layout stage.
The routing stage.
The translation stage.
The optimization stage.

explanation.correct: Routing is the stage that makes a circuit satisfy the coupling map: when a two-qubit gate is requested between non-adjacent physical qubits, the router inserts SWAP gates to bring the qubit states together. Layout only assigns the initial virtual->physical mapping.

distractors.A: The layout stage chooses which physical qubit each virtual qubit starts on; it does not insert SWAPs to move them during the circuit.
distractors.C: Translation rewrites gates into the target's basis set; it has nothing to do with connectivity.
distractors.D: Optimization simplifies and cancels gates; SWAP insertion for connectivity is the router's job, not optimization's.

questions.stem: Why must an abstract quantum circuit be transpiled before it can be run on IBM Quantum hardware through the Runtime primitives?

options.text: Hardware executes only ISA circuits: gates must be expressed in the device's native basis, and every two-qubit gate must act on physically-connected qubits — transpilation rewrites and routes the circuit to satisfy both.
Transpilation is optional; the V2 primitives silently convert any abstract circuit at submission time, so it only saves a little runtime.
Transpilation is needed only to draw the circuit nicely; execution accepts any circuit unchanged.
Transpilation exists to convert the circuit into OpenQASM 2, the only format the hardware accepts.

explanation.correct: Real devices have a fixed native gate set and a limited qubit connectivity. Transpilation maps virtual qubits to physical ones, routes two-qubit gates onto connected pairs (inserting SWAPs), and translates gates into the basis set — producing an ISA circuit, which is what the V2 primitives require as input.

distractors.B: The V2 primitives do NOT silently transpile arbitrary circuits; they expect ISA circuits and will error on non-ISA input. Transpiling is a required step, not a mere optimization.
distractors.C: Drawing has nothing to do with it; an un-transpiled circuit generally cannot execute on hardware at all.
distractors.D: Hardware execution is not about OpenQASM 2; the requirement is native basis gates plus valid connectivity (an ISA circuit).

questions.stem: Qubit 0 is flipped to `|1⟩`. The measurements deliberately map q0 -> c1 and q1 -> c0. What bitstring (order `c1 c0`) is observed on every shot?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2, 2)
qc.x(0)
qc.measure(0, 1)   # qubit 0 -> classical bit 1
qc.measure(1, 0)   # qubit 1 -> classical bit 0

explanation.correct: Qubit 0 is `1`, qubit 1 is `0`. The mapping stores qubit 0's result in c1 and qubit 1's result in c0, so c1=1 and c0=0. Printed as `c1 c0`, that is `10`.

distractors.A: `01` assumes the default mapping q0->c0, q1->c1; here the mapping is deliberately swapped, sending qubit 0's value to c1.
distractors.B: `11` would require both qubits to be 1, but only qubit 0 was flipped.
distractors.D: `00` would require both qubits to be 0; qubit 0 carries an X.

evidence.B: option='11'; observed 0/1000; full={'10': 1000} -> False
evidence.C: option='10'; observed 1000/1000; full={'10': 1000} -> True
evidence.D: option='00'; observed 0/1000; full={'10': 1000} -> False

questions.stem: Circuit `a` applies X to its single qubit; circuit `b` applies identity to its single qubit. Which two-qubit state does `a.tensor(b)` prepare (bit order `q1 q0`)?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

a = QuantumCircuit(1)
a.x(0)      # a's qubit -> |1>

b = QuantumCircuit(1)
b.id(0)     # b's qubit -> |0>

combined = a.tensor(b)
sv = Statevector(combined)

options.text: `|01⟩` — `b` (identity) lands on the higher-order qubit and `a` (X) on the lower one.
`|11⟩` — both qubits end up excited.
An equal superposition of all four basis states.
`|10⟩` — `a` occupies the higher-order qubit, so its X sets q1 to 1 while q0 stays 0.

explanation.correct: In `a.tensor(b)` the left operand `a` is placed on the MORE significant (higher-index) qubits and `b` on the less significant ones. So `a`'s X acts on q1 (giving 1) and `b`'s identity leaves q0 at 0, producing `|10⟩`.

distractors.A: `|01⟩` reverses the tensor convention; `a` (the left operand), not `b`, takes the higher-order qubit.
distractors.B: `b` applies identity, so q0 remains 0 — the state is not `|11⟩`.
distractors.C: Neither circuit contains an H or other superposition-creating gate, so the result is a definite basis state.

evidence.B: nonzero={'10': '(1+0j)'}; claim |11> -> False
evidence.C: nonzero={'10': '(1+0j)'} (1 states); claim superposition -> False
evidence.D: nonzero={'10': '(1+0j)'}; single |10> (a on high qubit) -> True

questions.stem: This code raises a `CircuitError` at the `measure` call. What is the fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)          # 2 qubits, 0 classical bits
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.measure([0, 1], [0, 1])     # CircuitError

options.text: The circuit has no classical bits; create it as `QuantumCircuit(2, 2)` (or add a `ClassicalRegister`) so there are bits to store the outcomes.
`measure` cannot take lists of qubits/bits; you must measure each qubit in a separate call.
The `measure` method does not exist; you must call `qc.measure_all()` instead.
The qubits must be measured before the CX gate, not after it.

explanation.correct: `QuantumCircuit(2)` creates 2 qubits and ZERO classical bits, so `measure` has nowhere to write. Declaring classical bits — `QuantumCircuit(2, 2)` or adding a `ClassicalRegister` — resolves it.

distractors.B: `measure` accepts parallel lists of qubits and clbits; the list form is fine once classical bits exist.
distractors.C: `measure` absolutely exists; `measure_all` is just a convenience that also creates its own register.
distractors.D: Gate ordering is irrelevant here — the error is the missing classical bits, not the position of the measurement.

evidence.A: bug=CircuitError: 'Index 0 out of range for size 0.'; with QuantumCircuit(2,2) measure([0,1],[0,1]) ran, counts={'11': 96, '00': 104} -> True
evidence.B: the list form measured 2 bits successfully once clbits existed -> 'lists not allowed' is False
evidence.C: QuantumCircuit.measure exists=True -> claim 'no measure method' is False
evidence.D: reordering the measurement does not add classical bits; irrelevant to the error -> False

questions.stem: What is the practical benefit of building a circuit from named registers such as `QuantumRegister(3, 'data')` and `QuantumRegister(1, 'anc')` instead of the bare `QuantumCircuit(4)` form?

options.text: Named registers make the circuit execute faster on hardware.
They let you address qubits and bits by meaningful groups and names (e.g. `data[0]`, `anc[0]`), improving readability and letting one circuit hold several logically-distinct registers.
Only qubits that belong to a named register can be measured; anonymous qubits cannot.
Named registers are required by the transpiler; a circuit built with `QuantumCircuit(4)` cannot be transpiled.

explanation.correct: Registers are an organizational tool. Naming them lets you reference qubits/bits by role (`data`, `anc`) and index within each group, which keeps larger circuits readable and lets one circuit contain multiple distinct registers. They carry no performance or execution requirement.

distractors.A: Register naming is purely structural metadata; it has no effect on execution speed.
distractors.C: Any qubit can be measured regardless of whether it lives in a user-named register; `QuantumCircuit(4)` qubits are measurable too.
distractors.D: Circuits built with the integer form transpile perfectly well; named registers are not a transpiler requirement.

questions.stem: A 3-parameter circuit is partially bound with a dict that supplies only `q`. How many free parameters does `partial` have, and which remain?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

p = Parameter('p'); q = Parameter('q'); r = Parameter('r')
qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.rx(p, 0)
qc.ry(q, 0)
qc.rz(r, 0)

partial = qc.assign_parameters({q: 1.0})

options.text: 0 — assigning any dict binds all of the circuit's parameters at once.
1 — only `q` remains free.
2 — `p` and `r` remain free; only `q` was bound.
It raises, because a dict must supply a value for every parameter.

explanation.correct: A dict binds exactly the parameters it names. Passing `{q: 1.0}` binds only `q`, leaving `p` and `r` free — so `partial.num_parameters` is 2. This is partial binding.

distractors.A: A dict binds only the keys you provide, not every parameter; the others stay symbolic.
distractors.B: `q` is the one that gets bound, so it is NOT among the remaining free parameters.
distractors.D: A dict is exactly how you do partial binding; it does not require a value for every parameter (a list would).

evidence.B: remaining=['p', 'r']; claim only q -> False
evidence.C: num_parameters=2, remaining=['p', 'r']; only q bound -> True
evidence.D: partial dict binding succeeded (remaining=['p', 'r']); claim 'raises' -> False

questions.stem: Select ALL true statements about parameterized circuits and how they interact with binding and transpilation.

options.text: The values in a LIST passed to `assign_parameters` are matched to `qc.parameters` in alphabetical name order, not in gate-insertion order.
A parameterized circuit can be transpiled first and have its parameters assigned afterward; the ISA circuit keeps the parameters until you bind them.
`assign_parameters` must be called before transpilation, otherwise the parameters are lost.
A dict passed to `assign_parameters` may bind only a subset of parameters (partial binding).
Reusing the same `Parameter` object on two gates raises an error.

explanation.correct: List binding follows `qc.parameters`, which is sorted alphabetically by name (A). Parameters survive transpilation, so you can transpile once and bind many times afterward (B). A dict binds only the named parameters (D).

distractors.C: Parameters are preserved through transpilation; you do NOT have to bind before transpiling (in fact binding after is the efficient pattern).
distractors.E: Reusing one `Parameter` across gates is fully supported — it ties those gates to a shared value, not an error.

evidence.B: transpiled ISA had 1 param(s); bound to 0 -> True
evidence.C: binding after transpile worked, so pre-transpile binding is not required -> False
evidence.D: dict {u:0.2} left 1 free -> True
evidence.E: reusing one Parameter on 2 gates gave num_parameters=1, raised=False

questions.stem: The SAME parameter `th` is used on two different gates. How many free parameters does `qc` have, and what happens when `th` is bound?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.circuit import Parameter

th = Parameter('th')
qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.rx(th, 0)
qc.rx(th, 1)

bound = qc.assign_parameters({th: 0.5})

options.text: `qc` has 2 free parameters — each gate gets its own independent copy of `th`.
`qc` has 1 free parameter, but binding sets only the first `rx`; the second stays symbolic.
It raises, because one `Parameter` object cannot be reused across two gates.
`qc` has 1 free parameter; binding `th` sets BOTH `rx` gates to 0.5 at once.

explanation.correct: A `Parameter` is a single symbolic object. Reusing `th` on both gates means `qc.num_parameters` is 1, and assigning `th` substitutes that one value into every gate that references it — so both `rx` gates become `0.5`. This is how weight-sharing ansätze tie gates together.

distractors.A: Reusing the same object does not clone it; there is one parameter, not two.
distractors.B: Binding a parameter updates every occurrence of it, not just the first gate.
distractors.C: Reusing a parameter across gates is fully valid and common; it does not raise.

evidence.B: after bind rx angles=[0.5, 0.5]; claim only first set -> False
evidence.C: reusing th did not raise (num_parameters=1) -> False
evidence.D: num_parameters=1; both rx angles=[0.5, 0.5] set to 0.5 -> True

questions.stem: Qubit 0 is set to `|1⟩` and measured into c0. A `switch` on c0 applies X to qubit 1 in `case(0)` and identity in the DEFAULT case. Qubit 1 is then measured. What outcome (order `c1 c0`) appears on every shot?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister

qr = QuantumRegister(2, 'q')
cr = ClassicalRegister(2, 'c')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, cr)
qc.x(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)
with qc.switch(cr[0]) as case:
    with case(0):
        qc.x(1)
    with case(case.DEFAULT):
        qc.id(1)
qc.measure(1, 1)

options.text: `01` on every shot — c0=1 takes the DEFAULT branch (identity), so qubit 1 stays 0.
`11` on every shot — the `case(0)` branch runs and applies X to qubit 1.
`10` on every shot.
A mix of `00` and `11`, roughly 50/50.

explanation.correct: c0 is deterministically 1 (qubit 0 was flipped). The `switch` matches value 1 against `case(0)` (no match) and falls to `case(DEFAULT)`, which applies identity, leaving qubit 1 at 0. So c1=0, c0=1 → `01` every shot.

distractors.B: `case(0)` only runs when c0=0; here c0=1, so the X branch is NOT taken.
distractors.C: `10` would require qubit 1 to be 1 while c0=0; neither holds.
distractors.D: There is no superposition — qubit 0 is a definite 1 — so the outcome is deterministic, not 50/50.

evidence.B: counts={'01': 1000}; all '11'? -> False
evidence.C: counts={'01': 1000}; all '10'? -> False
evidence.D: counts={'01': 1000}; mix of 00/11? -> False

questions.stem: Both qubits 0 and 1 are set to `|1⟩` and measured into the 2-bit register `c`. An `if_test` guarded by `expr.equal(c, 3)` flips qubit 2, which is read into register `o`. What value does `o` hold?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit, QuantumRegister, ClassicalRegister
from qiskit.circuit.classical import expr

qr = QuantumRegister(3, 'q')
c = ClassicalRegister(2, 'c')
o = ClassicalRegister(1, 'o')
qc = QuantumCircuit(qr, c, o)
qc.x(0); qc.x(1)
qc.measure(0, 0); qc.measure(1, 1)
with qc.if_test(expr.equal(c, 3)):
    qc.x(2)
qc.measure(2, o[0])

options.text: `o = 0` — `expr.equal` compares individual bits, so it can never match the whole-register value 3.
`o = 1` — `c` holds binary `11` = 3, so the condition is true and qubit 2 is flipped.
It raises — an `if_test` condition must be a `(register, value)` tuple; an `expr` is not accepted.
`o` is random 0 or 1 because qubit 2 was never given a definite initial state.

explanation.correct: `expr.equal(c, 3)` builds a classical expression that is true when the register `c`, interpreted as an integer, equals 3. With both bits set, `c` = binary `11` = 3, so the condition holds and X is applied to qubit 2, making `o` = 1.

distractors.A: `expr.equal` compares the whole register's integer value, not bit-by-bit; it matches 3 exactly here.
distractors.C: `if_test` accepts either a `(resource, value)` tuple OR a classical `expr`; passing `expr.equal(...)` is valid.
distractors.D: Qubit 2 starts in the definite state `|0⟩`; the conditional flips it deterministically, so `o` is not random.

evidence.B: counts={'1 11': 1000}; c=11=3 so condition fired, o always 1? -> True
evidence.C: if_test(expr.equal(...)) built and ran without error -> claim 'raises' is False
evidence.D: counts={'1 11': 1000}; o deterministic (not random) -> claim 'random' is False
